caption:
블로그에 RSS 피드 기능을 추가했습니다 📰

이제 Feedly, Inoreader 같은 RSS 리더로
새 글을 자동으로 받아볼 수 있어요.

이번 글에서는
👉 RSS 2.0 피드를 직접 구현한 과정
👉 한국어 / 영어 다국어 지원 방법
👉 한글 인코딩 깨짐 이슈와 해결 방법
👉 프론트엔드에서 RSS URL을 깔끔하게 노출하는 방법까지

실제 운영 중인 블로그 기준으로 정리했습니다.

블로그를 운영하고 있다면
RSS는 여전히 구독, SEO, 콘텐츠 배포의 기본 기능입니다.
“한 번 제대로 만들어두면 계속 쓰이는 기능”이기도 하고요.

🔗 전체 구현 과정과 코드는 블로그에서 확인하세요.
https://weggle-plus.co.kr/blog/posts/10

Still checking if your variables are null every two lines? 🤚🛑

Java 8 introduced Optional to help us deal with missing values without the dreaded NullPointerException. It’s not just a wrapper; it’s a way of writing “Functional” Java that is cleaner, safer, and much more readable.

Don’t use Optional.get()—it defeats the whole purpose! Always use .orElse(), .orElseThrow(), or .ifPresent().

caption:
🔥 Programming Mindset: Why Computer Needs Data Types

Hello Engineer !

Think about this

You see: 9876543210

You immediately know
👉 It’s a phone number
Not age.
Not price.

But for a computer,
it is just digits.

Unless we clearly tell:
“This is a phone number.”

That’s why computers need data types.
They don’t understand meaning —
they understand labels.

​🛑 STOP Writing "God Classes"! (Part 1/5: SOLID Principles)
​Welcome to my new series: Mastering SOLID in Java! 🚀
​Over the next few posts, we are going to break down the 5 essential design principles that turn "messy code" into "clean architecture."
​Today, we start with the 'S' — The Single Responsibility Principle (SRP).
​💡 The Rule: "A class should have one, and only one, reason to change."
​If your UserService is saving data to the database, sending welcome emails, AND generating PDF reports... you don't have a service, you have a monster! 🍝
​👉 Swipe through to see:
1️⃣ The Concept: Swiss Army Knife vs. Chef's Knife.
2️⃣ The Anti-Pattern: A "God Class" example (we've all seen this 😱).
3️⃣ The Solution: How to refactor it into clean, focused components.
​—
🧠 QUIZ TIME (Last Slide)
Check the code snippets in the final image.
​Which class violates the Single Responsibility Principle?
A) OrderCalculator
B) InvoicePrinter
C) ReportHandler
​Drop your answer (A, B, or C) in the comments! 👇
​🔔 Turn on notifications to catch Part 2: The Open-Closed Principle!
​.
.
.
#JavaDeveloper #CleanCode #SOLIDPrinciples #SoftwareArchitecture #Refactoring Java17 BackendDeveloper CodingBestPractices SoftwareEngineering DevCommunity

🚀 Starting my Data Engineering + DSA learning journey

Everyone talks about Data Science, but Data Engineering is what makes data usable.

Data Engineers work on:
✅ Collecting data
✅ Cleaning & transforming data
✅ Storing data
✅ Building reliable pipelines

I created this page to document what I learn daily — from basics to advanced — and to help others who are preparing for data roles and interviews.

If you’re learning SQL, Python, Big Data, or DSA, follow and grow with me 💪

Visualizing the C Memory Layout: The Virtual Address Space

Pointers and memory management are often the hardest hurdles for C programmers.

We write malloc(), we declare local variables, but where do they actually live inside the machine?

To help you dominate this forever and ever,
I created this diagram to visualize the "invisible" architecture of a running process.

Here is the breakdown of the map:

1. The Collision Course (Stack vs. Heap)

The most dramatic part of memory management is the dynamic gap in the middle.
The Heap (Top) grows UP via dynamic allocation.
The Stack (Middle) pushes DOWN via function calls (LIFO).

The Danger:

If they meet in the middle?
That is your Stack Overflow or OOM.

2. The "Bridge" (malloc)

Look at the arrow connecting the Stack to the Heap. This is the mental model you need:
Your pointer (void *ptr) lives on the Stack (automatically managed).
The data it points to (struct data) lives on the Heap (manually managed).

malloc() is the bridge that links these two worlds.

3. The Static Foundation

Code doesn't run in a vacuum.
The BSS and Data segments are set up by the OS (represented by Tux ) before main() even executes.
This ensures your globals and statics are zero-initialized and ready to go.

Why does this matter?

Understanding this layout isn't just trivia—it's how you debug segfaults, understand buffer overflows, and write safer systems code.

(If you found this visual helpful, feel free to save it as a reference!)

Help our C Programming Community: SHARE!

Day 12/90: Algorithms vs. Architecture. 🌳⚙️

Today was heavy on learning, but it was honestly a lot of fun.

The Theory: Spent time studying Tree Structures. I finally wrapped my head around the different traversal algorithms (Preorder, Inorder, Postorder) and how Binary Search Trees actually work under the hood.

The Build: Then I jumped back to continue Express.js.

The speed is insane compared to vanilla Node.

Learned to handle dynamic routes (using /:param).

Separated my logic into Routes and Controllers for cleaner code.

Connected SQLite (db.exec and .run) to actually store data.

Every day the full-stack picture gets a little clearer. 🧱

#90DaysOfCode #backenddeveloper #computerscience #expressjs #datastructures #coding #techjourney

Retries feel safe.
In production, they can be dangerous.

When a system is already struggling,
retries multiply the load and make recovery harder.

Design retries with:
• limits
• backoff
• context

Production teaches this the hard way.
